danger_signals.visual_form: chibi-styled Mad Gear gang members, childlike counterparts of arcade bosses
danger_signals.meaning: immediate combat threat; enemy positions indicate danger zones in level

danger_signals.visual_form: larger, distinct character sprites (Thrasher, Masamune, Abigail, Murasame); Cyborg Belger with mechanical enhancements
danger_signals.meaning: escalated threat level; stage-culminating danger requiring tactical play

danger_signals.visual_form: knives, shurikens, mallets positioned in level space as collectibles
danger_signals.meaning: incoming combat intensity; player should prepare for heightened danger

danger_signals.visual_form: vitality bar reduction visible during combat
danger_signals.meaning: player vulnerability increasing; need for food power-ups or defensive play

safety_signals.visual_form: vitality-restoring food items scattered in environment
safety_signals.meaning: safe interaction opportunity; restores player health and morale

safety_signals.visual_form: extra life power-ups positioned in level
safety_signals.meaning: safety buffer; reduces consequence of failure

safety_signals.visual_form: additional continue chances available as collectibles
safety_signals.meaning: world-state shows player has second-chance opportunities post-failure

safety_signals.visual_form: stage progression interface, narrative text boxes between levels
safety_signals.meaning: safe transition zone; story advancement indicates stability and progress

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: gang membership implied through enemy sprite design and narrative context
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Thrasher (Stage 1), Masamune (Stage 2), Abigail (Stage 3), Murasame (Stage 4), Cyborg Belger (Stage 5 final boss)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: urban Metro City locations; street-gang controlled territories; mechanized final boss suggests technological corruption of gang leadership

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: characterized by distinct martial identities (Mayor Haggar, Cody, Guy)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: player character selection represents competing legitimate response to gang threat; three distinct fighting styles (wrestling, martial arts, ninjutsu) show institutional/personal opposition to Mad Gear

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: enemy gangs occupy stage environments; Mad Gear members present as primary environmental hazards
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies patrol and attack player; boss encounters block stage progression, suggesting territorial dominance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: narrative text boxes establish Jessica's abduction by Belger; stage progression framed as rescue mission
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player objective remains constant across all five stages; bosses guard passage forward, indicating Jessica held deeper in Mad Gear territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: skill level system with six tiers; character sprite may show visual feedback at higher levels; new special moves unlock at Level 4
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player gains Tornado Sweep (Cody), Tornado Kick (Guy), or Running Clothesline (Haggar); improved attack power and vitality indicate growing strength against gang

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: stage progression from street encounters to boss lairs; final boss is 'mechanically enhanced' Cyborg Belger—tech integration signals escalating danger
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy variety increases; boss encounters become harder; final mechanical transformation of main antagonist represents corrupted leadership state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cody and Guy start at skill Level 1; Haggar starts at Level 3—visual/mechanical difference in starting power
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Haggar has initial advantage reflecting wrestler background; other characters must train (gain EXP) to match his starting competency

environmental_storytelling.example: five stages represent geographic progression through Metro City toward final confrontation with Belger; each stage is distinct location reinforcing narrative geography
Cyborg Belger's mechanical enhancement visualizes corruption and technological perversion of gang leadership; contrasts with organic gang member designs earlier in game
Cody gets knife (street fighter), Guy gets shurikens (ninja identity), Haggar gets mallet (wrestler tool); weapons reinforce character class identity and martial philosophy
chibi/super-deformed art style reframes serious arcade narrative as satirical; childlike character design softens violence while maintaining beat-em-up mechanics
EXP system tied to finishing move quality; stronger moves grant more XP, signaling skill hierarchy and combat mastery progression through visual feedback

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: in Japanese version, answers 'no' to his prompts cause flinching and health damage; in Western version, reduced to two prompts instead of three—mechanical humiliation of boss through dialogue choice shows player agency over seemingly dominant antagonist

offers true/false battle-of-wits trivia; correct answers grant extra continue; wrong answer ends quiz and proceeds to fight—Japanese version creates conditional non-combat path reflecting Abigail's intellectual vanity
visual/mechanical improvement in attack power and vitality as skill level increases; Level 4 unlock triggers new special move animation—growth manifests as both visual feedback and expanded moveset
enemies positioned throughout stage environment as patrol/occupation forces; boss encounters at stage-end function as territorial gatekeepers preventing forward progress
mechanical enhancement distinguishes final boss from standard gang members; transformation signals culmination of threat and corruption arc—technology integration reflects compromised leadership

notes: Mighty Final Fight uses chibi/super-deformed aesthetic to reframe serious arcade narrative as comedic satire. Danger and faction communication relies on enemy sprite design, boss encounters as territorial markers, and stage progression geography. World-state manifests through skill leveling system (player power growth), boss mechanical enhancement (escalating threat), and narrative positioning of Jessica's location (adventure structure). Regional differences (Japanese quiz mechanic, Western dialogue reductions) affect how boss psychology is communicated. Character weapon identity and starting skill levels signal martial philosophy and competency hierarchy. No explicit color-coding or sigils documented for factions; identity communication relies on narrative context and character design.
